description: The figure presents the temperature dependence of compressive yield stress (0–800 °C) for Fe-26Al-based alloys, divided into two sub-panels. Panel (a) compares binary Fe-26Al with Ti- and V-containing alloys (Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C), while panel (b) compares the binary alloy with Nb- and Ta-containing alloys (Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C). All curves exhibit an anomalous yield-strength peak at intermediate temperatures. The Ti- and V-bearing alloys in (a) show a peak near 550 °C at approximately 400 MPa. In (b), the Nb- and Ta alloys display a peak shifted to lower temperatures and higher stresses relative to the binary alloy; Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C reaches the highest peak at 474 °C and 619 MPa. These trends demonstrate that precipitation strengthening by MC carbides and Laves phase (Fe,Al)2Nb markedly enhances high-temperature strength, with the Nb-modified alloy exhibiting the strongest resistance to thermal softening across the tested temperature range.
